qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Password reset endpoint returns the live reset token to unauthenticated callers
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding is an account-takeover flaw in the password-reset flow and does not involve the advisory's GET /images SVG-processing denial-of-service path.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Google OAuth login callback accepts attacker-supplied codes without any state binding
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding concerns CSRF/state binding in the optional Google OAuth login extension, not the advisory's SVG handling in GET /images.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Public image proxy allows unauthenticated memory and CPU exhaustion
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding targets the same public GET /images endpoint, but its preserved evidence identifies a different root cause: unbounded external fetches, missing size/time/content checks, and Sharp rasterization with limitInputPixels disabled, rather than the advisory's specific failure to limit SVG use-element shadow tree height or pattern tile dimensions.

qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Stripe payment intent creation lets a cheap cart pay a different expensive order
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: This finding is a payment/order integrity flaw in the Stripe integration and is unrelated to the advisory's GET /images SVG-processing denial of service.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described vulnerable code or root cause. The closest finding, public_image_proxy_dos, matches the endpoint and broad DoS class but not the advisory's specific SVG use-element shadow tree or pattern tile limit failure.
evidence: The advisory describes unauthenticated denial of service in GET /images caused by failing to limit the height of the SVG use-element shadow tree or the dimensions of pattern tiles during SVG processing.

public_image_proxy_dos instead describes a different source-to-sink path: attacker-controlled external URLs are fetched with response.arrayBuffer() without response-size or timeout limits, then passed to Sharp; the preserved evidence also points to Sharp SVG handling with limitInputPixels disabled.

The other qualifying findings concern password-reset token disclosure, Google OAuth login CSRF, and Stripe order/payment integrity, which are unrelated to the advisory's affected component and root cause.
